chunks.text: Four pests cause 90% of houseplant pest problems indoors. They all spread from one plant to another, so when you find any of them, isolate the infested plant from the rest.

Tiny (0.5mm) arachnids — not insects. The clearest signal is fine webbing between leaves and stems. They thrive in dry air and quickly destroy plants in heated winter homes.

- **What you see**: stippled, dusty-looking leaves; fine webbing on the underside; yellow speckles that look almost gold-flecked
- **Where they hide**: underside of leaves, leaf axils
- **What to do**: shower the plant in lukewarm water (literal shower — they hate water), every 3 days for 2 weeks. Add neem oil or insecticidal soap between showers. Raise the humidity to make the environment hostile.

Spider mites are the hardest pest to eradicate. You will think you got them; you didn't. Treat for a full 3 weeks after the last sighting.

Small white cottony-looking insects that cluster in leaf joints. They suck sap and excrete a sticky honeydew that grows sooty mold.

- **What you see**: white fluff in the crooks of stems and leaves, leaves looking sticky or shiny, ants crawling on the plant (they farm the honeydew)
- **What to do**: spot-treat with a Q-tip dipped in rubbing alcohol. The alcohol dissolves their waxy coating instantly; they're dead the moment you touch them. Follow up with neem oil weekly until you've gone 3 weeks without seeing one.

Mealybugs can be dormant in soil too. Check the root crown when repotting.

Tiny black flies that crawl across the soil surface and buzz around when you water. The adults are annoying but harmless; the larvae eat root hairs.

- **What you see**: small black flies emerging when you water; the larvae are translucent worms 2-4mm long in the top inch of soil
- **What to do**: let the top inch of soil dry out completely between waterings (the larvae need moisture). Yellow sticky traps for the adults. For a heavy infestation, drench the soil with mosquito bits (BTI) — kills the larvae over a week.

Bottom-watering works very well against fungus gnats since the top stays dry.

The hardest pest to identify because they're tiny and elusive. Slim, fast-moving insects that scrape leaves and suck the sap, leaving silvery streaks and tiny black dots (their feces).

- **What you see**: silvery or bronze streaks on leaves; tiny black flecks; distorted new growth; leaves that look "scratched"
- **What to do**: heavy infestations need systemic insecticides (look for imidacloprid) or repeated applications of spinosad. Less-bad infestations: weekly insecticidal soap + sticky traps + clean off affected leaves.

Thrips can reproduce parthenogenically and have a fast life cycle. Don't stop treatment after one week.

- **Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks** before introducing them to your collection. Most pest problems trace back to a new arrival.
- **Inspect undersides of leaves** when you water. That's where most pests hide.
- **Don't mist if you have spider mites** — water alone isn't enough and you'll spread spores. Use insecticidal soap instead.
- **Isolate infested plants** to a different room until 3 weeks past the last sighting.

Houseplants need fertilizer because their soil is a closed system — unlike outdoor soil, nothing's replenishing the nutrients as the plant uses them. But fertilizer is overdone far more often than underdone, and burned roots from over-fertilizing look a lot like underwatering.

- **Growing season (spring + summer)**: every 2-4 weeks at half the recommended strength.
- **Winter**: don't fertilize, or once every 6-8 weeks at quarter strength. Most houseplants slow down or go dormant when light drops.
- **Newly potted plants**: don't fertilize for 6-8 weeks after repotting. Fresh potting mix has nutrients already; adding more burns the still-recovering roots.
- **Stressed plants**: never fertilize a plant that's wilting, recently moved, or showing signs of root rot. Fix the underlying problem first.

The three numbers on every fertilizer label: nitrogen-phosphorus-potassium.

- **N (nitrogen)**: leafy growth. Most houseplants are leaf-focused so a higher first number is good.
- **P (phosphorus)**: roots + flowers. Important when flowering or rooting cuttings.
- **K (potassium)**: overall plant vigor, disease resistance.

A balanced fertilizer (10-10-10 or 20-20-20) works for most houseplants. Specialty plants want specialty ratios — orchids want a 30-10-10 in growing season, then 10-10-30 to trigger blooming. Succulents want a low-nitrogen mix (like 2-7-7) so they don't get leggy.

Almost every houseplant fertilizer label tells you to dilute to about 1 tsp per gallon (or whatever). Houseplants almost always do better at half that. The label is written assuming you're fertilizing tomatoes outside.

Half-strength every 2 weeks > full-strength every 4. Steady mild feeding beats infrequent shock feeding.

- Pale leaves, especially new growth
- Slow or stalled growth despite good light + water
- Lower leaves yellowing while the rest of the plant looks fine
- Plant hasn't been fertilized in 6+ months

- Crispy brown leaf tips (often confused with low humidity)
- White crust on the soil surface or pot rim (mineral salt buildup)
- Burnt-looking leaf edges
- Leaves dropping with no obvious other cause

If you suspect over-fertilizing, flush the soil: pour water through the pot 3-4 times the pot's volume to wash out salts. Skip the next 2 fertilizer cycles.

Synthetic liquid fertilizers (Schultz, Miracle-Gro) act fast and are predictable. Organic options (worm castings, fish emulsion, compost tea) are gentler and slower. For indoor use synthetic is fine — organic fertilizers can smell, attract fungus gnats, and need to be turned into the soil.

Slow-release pellets (Osmocote) work for 3-6 months. Convenient for plants you'd otherwise forget to fertilize, but they release nutrients faster in heat — be careful in summer.
Most tropical houseplants come from rainforests where humidity sits at 70-90%. Indoor air, especially in winter with the heat running, is often 20-30%. That's desert humidity.

chunks.sectionTitle: Which plants actually need high humidity
chunks.text:
- **Calatheas, marantas, ctenanthes (prayer plants)**: 50%+ minimum, ideally 60-70%. They crisp up below 40%.
- **Ferns** (Boston, maidenhair, bird's nest): 50%+. Maidenhairs are notorious — they want 70%+ and turn to dust below that.
- **Anthuriums, alocasias**: 60%+.
- **Orchids** (most): 50-70%.
- **Calathea ornata, Calathea white fusion**: cry for the swamp; 70%+.

chunks.sectionTitle: Plants that don't care about humidity
chunks.text:
- **Snake plants, ZZ plants, pothos, philodendrons, monsteras**: tolerate 30-40% just fine.
- **Cacti, succulents, jade**: prefer it dry.

If you're a beginner, start with plants that don't care about humidity. The plants on the "needs high humidity" list will frustrate you in a normal home until you've built up infrastructure.

In order of effectiveness:

1. **Run a humidifier**. The only method that actually works in a dry room. A $40 cool-mist humidifier can hold a 200 sq ft room at 50-60%. Run it on a timer with the plants.
2. **Group plants together**. Plants transpire moisture from their leaves. A cluster of plants creates a microclimate noticeably wetter than the surrounding room.
3. **Move plants to the bathroom**. Bathrooms with a shower run a regular humidity cycle. Anthuriums and calatheas often do best on a bathroom shelf.
4. **Pebble trays**. A tray of pebbles with water under each pot. Adds maybe 5-10% locally. Marginal but free.
5. **Misting**. Mostly theater. The humidity bump lasts a few minutes. Doesn't hurt if you enjoy it but won't save a calathea.
A $10 digital hygrometer from a hardware store. Put it next to the plants you care about. Most homes are drier than people guess — getting an actual number is more useful than misting "just in case".

- Crispy brown leaf tips, especially on calatheas
- Crispy edges curling inward
- Older fern fronds turning brown and crumbling
- Failure of new leaves to unfurl fully

Brown tips that look like they're literally sandpaper-textured are almost always humidity, not water. Crank up the humidifier before reaching for the watering can.
Light is the single most under-appreciated houseplant variable. Most "I'm not sure why my plant is dying" problems are actually light problems.

- **Bright indirect**: within a few feet of a sunny window, but not in the path of direct beams. Most tropicals (monsteras, philodendrons, pothos, peace lilies, calatheas).
- **Direct sun**: in the path of sun for at least several hours a day. Most succulents, cacti, citrus, jade, string of pearls.
- **Medium light**: 5-10 feet from a sunny window, or a bright north-facing window. ZZ plants, snake plants, cast iron plants, philodendrons (they tolerate this even though they prefer bright indirect).
- **Low light**: more than 10 feet from any window, or a windowless bathroom. Very few plants actually thrive in true low light — pothos and snake plants tolerate it but grow slowly and stretch.

Stand where your plant lives. If you can read a book without artificial light, it's at least medium. If you have to squint, it's low. If you can see a sharp shadow of your hand on the wall, it's direct sun.

A cheap light meter (or smartphone app) gives lux readings. Rough guidance:

- 200-500 lux: low
- 500-2000 lux: medium
- 2000-10000 lux: bright indirect
- 10000+ lux: direct sun

- **South-facing**: brightest, most direct sun all day. Great for succulents, cacti, citrus. Tropicals burn here unless filtered with a sheer curtain.
- **East-facing**: gentle morning sun, ideal for most tropicals.
- **West-facing**: hot afternoon sun. Tougher on sensitive plants but good for medium-light lovers and succulents.
- **North-facing**: indirect light all day, no direct sun. Best for shade-tolerant plants.

- Pale or yellowing new growth
- Leggy, stretched stems reaching toward the window
- Small leaves on new growth
- Slow or no growth
- Variegated plants losing their variegation (going all-green)

- Scorched brown patches on leaves (especially on the side facing the window)
- Faded or washed-out leaf color
- Crispy, curled leaf edges
- Wilting that doesn't recover after watering

If your apartment has no good window, a basic full-spectrum LED grow light from any hardware store solves the problem. Run 10-12 hours a day on a timer. Keep it 12-18 inches above the plant. Most tropicals are happy under a $30 grow light.
Propagation is how you turn one plant into many. For most popular houseplants it's straightforward and forgiving — even beginners get a high success rate.
Spring through early fall, when the parent plant is actively growing. Cuttings root much faster in warm weather with long days. Winter cuttings often sit for weeks before rooting.

chunks.sectionTitle: Stem cuttings — the most common method
chunks.text:
Works for: pothos, philodendrons, monsteras, hoyas, tradescantia, peperomia, ficus, jade, most tropical climbers.

1. **Find a node.** A node is the spot on the stem where a leaf attaches; it's where roots will grow from. You need at least one node, ideally two.
2. **Cut just below a node** with clean scissors. The cutting should have 2-4 leaves and at least one bare node.
3. **Remove the lowest leaf** so its node sits in water/soil without leaf rot.
4. **Choose a rooting medium**:
   - **Water**: clearest results, you can see the roots. Change water every 3-5 days. Transition to soil once roots are 2-3 inches.
   - **Damp sphagnum moss**: faster than water for many aroids. Keep in a clear container so you can watch progress.
   - **Direct to soil**: skip the water-rooting step. Stick cutting into damp well-draining soil, cover with a clear bag for humidity, place in indirect light.
5. **Wait**. Most cuttings root in 2-4 weeks. Some (monsteras, fiddle leaf figs) take 4-8.

Works for: snake plants, jade, African violets, peperomia, some succulents (echeverias, sedums).

For most leaf cuttings: just lay or stand the leaf on damp soil/perlite, keep barely moist, and a new plantlet emerges from the base in a few weeks. The original leaf eventually dies off after the baby is established.

Note: variegated snake plants and variegated monsteras propagated from leaf cuttings usually revert to all-green. Stem cuttings preserve variegation.

Works for: ZZ plants, snake plants, spider plants, calatheas, peace lilies, ferns, anything that grows in clumps.

When you unpot one of these and see multiple distinct plants sharing soil, you can separate them into individual pots. Each section needs roots + at least one growth point.

Best done when repotting anyway. Tease the root mass apart, cut through any sections you can't pull apart, repot each piece in its own appropriately-sized pot.

For plants too large to take cuttings from without damage (fiddle leaf figs, big rubber plants). You wound the stem partway up, wrap damp moss + plastic around the wound, and roots grow into the moss while the cutting is still attached to the parent. Once roots form, cut below the moss and pot it up.

More work than a stem cutting but very high success rate for difficult plants.

- **Cutting rotted in water**: water was stagnant or the cutting had no clean node. Change water more often; cut a fresh piece below the rotted part if salvageable.
- **No roots after a month**: light is too low (move to brighter indirect spot) or temperature is too cold (warm conditions speed rooting).
- **Roots formed but leaves yellowed when transferred to soil**: water roots and soil roots are structurally different; the cutting got overwhelmed. Acclimate slower — keep soil very moist for the first 2 weeks then taper.

chunks.sectionTitle: What you can sell, trade, or give away
chunks.text: A well-rooted cutting is a real gift. People love receiving propagated plants — there's a community around it (plant swaps, local groups). It's also a good way to test new soil mixes or care approaches on copies of your favorite plants before doing it to the original.
Repotting is less frequent than people assume. Most houseplants need it every 1-3 years, not every season. And bigger isn't always better — going up too many sizes is a fast way to cause root rot.

Repot when:

- Roots are growing out the drainage holes
- Water runs straight through the pot and out the bottom (the root ball has displaced the soil)
- The plant has stopped growing despite good light + water
- You see roots circling around the inside of the pot when you slide it out
- The soil compacts down significantly between waterings (no aeration left)

DON'T repot when:

- The plant is flowering — wait until after
- The plant is stressed, sick, or recovering from anything
- It's mid-winter (most houseplants don't grow then)
- You just brought the plant home — wait 2-3 weeks for it to acclimate

Best season to repot is early spring, right before the growth spurt.

Go up by one pot size. A 4" plant goes into a 5" or 6" pot, not a 10". Excess soil holds excess water around roots that can't reach it; that water sits stagnant and breeds rot.

The new pot should be 1-2 inches wider in diameter than the old one. That's it.

1. **Water the plant a day before** repotting. A hydrated root ball comes out cleanly; a dry one shatters.
2. **Prepare the new pot**: drainage hole confirmed, a thin layer of fresh soil at the bottom.
3. **Slide the plant out** of the old pot by tilting it sideways and squeezing the sides. Don't yank.
4. **Loosen the root ball gently**. If roots are tightly wound, tease them apart with your fingers — it stimulates outward growth into the new soil. Trim any dead/brown roots.
5. **Set the plant in the new pot** at the same depth it was in the old one. Don't bury the crown.
6. **Fill in with fresh soil** around the sides. Press lightly — don't pack hard. You want soil contact, not compacted soil.
7. **Water thoroughly**, let it drain. Wait at least 4 weeks before fertilizing.

Don't use generic "potting soil" straight from the bag for most houseplants — it's too dense. A good basic mix:

- 60% potting soil
- 30% perlite or pumice (for drainage + aeration)
- 10% orchid bark or coco coir (for chunkier structure)

Adjustments by plant type:

- **Succulents/cacti**: 40% potting soil + 50% perlite + 10% coarse sand. Drains in seconds.
- **Aroids (monsteras, philodendrons)**: add more orchid bark — they're epiphytes and like chunky, airy mix.
- **African violets**: pre-mixed African violet soil is fine; it's a fluffier blend than standard.
- **Orchids**: never use potting soil. Use bark-based orchid mix.

The plant may droop for a few days. That's normal — root disturbance temporarily reduces water uptake. Don't compensate with more water.

Hold off on fertilizer for 4-6 weeks. Fresh soil has enough nutrients to start. Hold off on direct sun for a week.

If a leaf or two yellows in the first week, that's also normal stress shedding. Let it run its course.

chunks.articleTitle: Root rot — how to spot it and what to do
chunks.sectionTitle: Root rot — how to spot it and what to do
chunks.text: Root rot is the silent killer of houseplants. By the time the symptoms are obvious aboveground, the roots are often 80% gone. Catching it early is the difference between a save and a goodbye.

chunks.articleTitle: Root rot — how to spot it and what to do
chunks.text:
Root rot is a fungal infection (most commonly *Pythium* or *Phytophthora*) that thrives in waterlogged soil with low oxygen. It eats the roots from the tips upward. Once it reaches the crown, the plant is unrecoverable.

The fungi are everywhere — in the air, the soil, on your fingers. They're harmless until conditions favor them: too much water, too little drainage, soil that stays soggy for days.

chunks.articleTitle: Root rot — how to spot it and what to do
chunks.text:
Aboveground:

- Drooping despite wet soil (the classic tell)
- Yellow leaves, especially lower ones
- A musty, rotten smell from the soil
- Soft, mushy stem at the soil line
- Stunted or stopped growth

Belowground (you have to unpot to see):

- Healthy roots: white or cream, firm, branching
- Rotted roots: brown or black, mushy, easy to crush between your fingers, often smell foul
- Root ball is wet days after watering

chunks.articleTitle: Root rot — how to spot it and what to do
chunks.sectionTitle: How to save a plant with root rot
chunks.text:
1. **Unpot immediately**. Don't wait. Every day the rot spreads.
2. **Rinse the root ball** under tepid water. Be gentle but thorough — you need to see what you're working with.
3. **Cut off every rotten root.** Use clean scissors (wipe with rubbing alcohol between cuts). Trim back to white, firm tissue. Don't be precious — leaving even a small amount of rot reintroduces it.
4. **Trim foliage proportionally.** If you removed 50% of the roots, remove 30-50% of the leaves so the remaining roots aren't overwhelmed.
5. **Repot in fresh, dry, well-draining soil.** Don't reuse the old soil — it's infected. Use a small pot — over-potting is what caused this in the first place.
6. **Water sparingly** for the next few weeks. Wait until the top half of the soil is dry. The plant has less root mass and can't process as much water.

Survival rate depends on how much healthy root remained. >50% healthy roots: usually recovers. <30%: long shot, but propagation from a stem cutting may save the genetics.

chunks.articleTitle: Root rot — how to spot it and what to do
chunks.text:
- **Use a pot with drainage holes.** Always. Decorative no-hole pots are death traps.
- **Use well-draining soil.** Most generic "potting mix" is too dense for houseplants. Add 30-50% perlite or pumice.
- **Don't water on a schedule.** Water when the soil is dry, not when the calendar says so.
- **Don't size up the pot dramatically.** A 4" plant should go into a 5" or 6" pot, not a 10". Excess soil holds excess water.
- **Don't leave saucers full.** Empty them after watering.

chunks.articleTitle: Root rot — how to spot it and what to do
chunks.text: If the stem at the soil line is mushy and you can pull the plant out of the soil with no resistance, the crown is gone. Take a cutting from healthy upper growth (if any) and propagate; compost the rest.

Most houseplants come from places without seasons, but indoor plants still experience seasons because of light and temperature shifts in your home. The two big inflection points are mid-fall and mid-spring.

What changes:

- Daylight drops 30-50% between September and December
- Indoor heating drops humidity dramatically (often into the 20s%)
- Plants enter slower growth or dormancy
- Cold drafts from windows become a real risk

What to adjust:

- **Water less often.** Plants in low-growth mode use less water. Wait longer between waterings. A monstera watered weekly in July might want every 2-3 weeks in November.
- **Stop fertilizing** after mid-October. Most plants won't use it and it'll just burn roots.
- **Move plants away from cold windows.** A 2-inch buffer from a single-pane window is enough to avoid the cold draft that drops leaf surface temperature into the 40s overnight.
- **Add a humidifier** for tropicals. The combination of forced-air heat and closed windows wrecks humidity-sensitive plants.
- **Move sun-loving plants closer to windows.** South-facing windows that were too hot in summer are perfect in winter.

The hardest season for tropicals. Expect:

- Slower growth or none at all (normal — don't panic)
- More leaf drop than usual (also often normal)
- Plants near windows getting cold damage on leaves touching the glass
- Higher pest pressure, especially spider mites (love dry winter air)

What to do:

- **Inspect for pests weekly.** Catching mites at week 1 vs week 4 is the difference between a quick recovery and a slow death.
- **Don't repot or propagate.** Wait for spring.
- **Consider grow lights** if your plants are clearly straining. Most tropicals tolerate the dim months but a few hours of supplemental light a day prevents leggy stretch.

The opposite of fall. Plants wake up.

- **Resume fertilizing** at half-strength as you see new growth.
- **Increase watering frequency** as the plant accelerates.
- **Repot** anything that needs it before the growth spurt hits in earnest.
- **Propagate** — spring cuttings root the fastest.
- **Watch for leggy stretching** that happened over winter and consider pruning to encourage bushier regrowth.

chunks.sectionTitle: Summer (peak growth, watch for sun + heat)
chunks.text:
- **Water more often** — pots dry out fast in heat.
- **Watch for sunburn** on plants near south-facing windows; consider a sheer curtain.
- **Fertilize regularly** — every 2-3 weeks.
- **Plants moved outside** for the summer need a slow acclimation. A week in deep shade, then a week in dappled shade, then partial sun. Don't dump an indoor plant into 6 hours of direct sun on day one.

chunks.sectionTitle: Plants that should move outside in summer
chunks.text:
- Citrus, hibiscus, gardenias, jasmines: thrive outdoors in summer humidity + light.
- Outdoor-style tropicals (bird of paradise, banana plants): same.

- Calatheas, marantas, ferns: outdoor wind + variable humidity stresses them.
- Variegated plants: direct sun can burn variegation.
- Sensitive succulents: most actually prefer indoor controlled conditions to unpredictable outdoor weather.

chunks.sectionTitle: Bringing plants back inside in fall
chunks.text: Inspect for pests before they come in. Hose off the foliage, check the underside of every leaf, and drench the soil with neem if you suspect anything. One infested plant brought inside in October can spread mites across your collection by January.

chunks.articleTitle: Brown leaf tips — what they actually mean
chunks.sectionTitle: Brown leaf tips — what they actually mean
chunks.text: Brown leaf tips are the second-most-common houseplant complaint after yellow leaves. They're almost always one of three things, and they're almost never caused by the thing people guess first (overwatering).

chunks.articleTitle: Brown leaf tips — what they actually mean
chunks.text:
Plants from rainforest environments crisp at the tips when ambient humidity drops below ~40%. The tips dry out faster than the plant can rehydrate them.

Most likely culprits:

- Calatheas, marantas, prayer plants: even 50% humidity isn't enough; they want 60-70%
- Ferns: maidenhairs are notorious; Boston ferns less so but still demanding
- Dracaenas, especially Janet Craig and Lemon Lime
- Spider plants in winter
- Peace lilies in dry heating-season air

The tell: tips are crispy-dry like paper, not soft. The brown is hard, not mushy. The edges may also be crispy a bit further in.

Fix: humidifier. Mist trays and misting don't really work for this; the only reliable solution is to add water vapor to the air with a humidifier set to 50%+.

chunks.articleTitle: Brown leaf tips — what they actually mean
chunks.sectionTitle: Cause 2: Salt buildup in soil (water quality)
chunks.text:
Tap water with high mineral content (chlorine, fluoride, dissolved solids) leaves residue in the soil. The roots can't filter all of it, so excess salts move up into the leaves and concentrate at the tips, burning them.

Most likely if:

- You water with cold tap water in a hard-water area
- You fertilize regularly without flushing the pot
- The pot has a white crust on the rim or soil surface
- Multiple plants are getting brown tips at once

Fix: flush the soil. Run water through the pot 3-4 times its volume to wash out accumulated salts. Switch to filtered water or rainwater. Skip 1-2 fertilizer cycles. Re-flush every 3-6 months.

Particularly sensitive plants: calatheas, dracaenas, spider plants, prayer plants. These want rainwater or filtered water, not tap.

chunks.articleTitle: Brown leaf tips — what they actually mean
chunks.text:
Letting a plant go bone-dry between waterings, then drenching it, then going bone-dry again — the swings damage root tips, and the damage shows up as leaf-tip dieback.

The tell: tips are brown but the rest of the leaf looks healthy. The plant gets browning tips every few weeks despite no obvious cause.

Fix: be more consistent. Water before the plant goes completely dry. Use a moisture meter if you can't read the soil reliably. For sensitive plants, bottom watering is more consistent than top watering.

chunks.articleTitle: Brown leaf tips — what they actually mean
chunks.text:
Fertilizer salts are the same problem as tap-water salts, just from a different source. The symptoms and fix are identical to cause 2.

If you fertilize at full strength weekly, that's the problem. Drop to half strength every 2-3 weeks and flush the pot.

chunks.articleTitle: Brown leaf tips — what they actually mean
chunks.sectionTitle: What to do with the brown tips
chunks.text:
Once a tip is brown, it's not coming back — the leaf can't re-green dead tissue. You have two options:

1. **Leave them alone** if it's just a couple of mm. The brown is harmless, just cosmetic.
2. **Trim them off** for appearance. Use clean scissors and cut to match the natural shape of the leaf. Some people prefer a flat cut; others prefer to mimic the leaf's natural taper. Don't cut into healthy tissue.

Either way, treat the underlying cause or the next leaves will do the same thing.

chunks.articleTitle: Brown leaf tips — what they actually mean
chunks.sectionTitle: When brown tips mean something worse
chunks.text: If the brown is spreading rapidly (more than a few mm per week), is soft/mushy rather than crispy, or is accompanied by yellow halos around the brown, you have a fungal/bacterial issue, not a humidity issue. Improve airflow, stop misting, water only at the soil line, and consider a copper-based fungicide if it doesn't resolve.

Yellow leaves are the most ambiguous signal on a houseplant. They can mean five different things, and the only way to narrow it down is to look at *which* leaves are yellow and *how* they got that way.

- **Most likely: overwatering**. Roots are suffocating; the plant sheds older leaves to conserve resources. Check the soil — if it's been wet for a week, you've overwatered. Let it dry out completely, then water less often going forward.
- **Also possible: natural shedding**. Mature plants drop their oldest leaves periodically. If it's one leaf a month and the rest of the plant looks happy, ignore it.

chunks.sectionTitle: Lower leaves, plant is also wilted, soil is dry
chunks.text: - **Likely: underwatering**. Older leaves go first when the plant is rationing. Water thoroughly and the wilt should resolve in a few hours.

- **Likely: nutrient deficiency**, usually nitrogen or iron. Fertilize at half-strength with a balanced houseplant fertilizer. If the issue is iron specifically (called "chlorosis" — yellow leaves with green veins), it's often a pH problem; switch to filtered water if you're on hard tap water.

chunks.sectionTitle: Yellow with brown spots or rings
chunks.text: - **Likely: a fungal or bacterial infection**, often caused by water sitting on leaves overnight. Move the plant to better airflow, stop misting, water only at the soil line.

chunks.sectionTitle: All-over yellowing in days, plant looks soft/limp
chunks.text: - **Likely: root rot from overwatering**, or temperature shock. Unpot and inspect roots — healthy roots are white/cream and firm; rotten ones are brown/black and mushy. Cut off the rot, repot in fresh soil, water sparingly.

chunks.sectionTitle: Yellow leaves on a plant you just bought
chunks.text: - **Likely: shipping stress** or moving from a greenhouse to your home's drier air. Wait two weeks before assuming anything is wrong. Don't repot a stressed plant.

Once a leaf has yellowed past 30-40%, it's not coming back. The plant can't re-green a leaf. Snip yellow leaves off at the base — it lets the plant put energy into new growth and looks better.

Don't pull. Cut. Pulling can damage the stem.
The biggest cause of houseplant death is overwatering, not underwatering. A wilted plant usually recovers within hours of being watered. A plant rotting from too much water often can't be saved.

chunks.sectionTitle: How to know when to water
chunks.text:
Stick your finger an inch into the soil. If it feels dry, water. If it's still moist, wait. For pots too deep to reach the root zone, use a wooden chopstick or skewer — push it in, pull it out, and check if soil sticks to it. Damp soil sticks; dry soil doesn't.

Weight is the second-best signal. A dry pot feels noticeably lighter than a freshly-watered one. After a few cycles you can tell whether a plant needs water just by lifting the pot.

Avoid watering on a fixed calendar schedule. The right interval changes with the season, your home's humidity, the size of the pot, and how root-bound the plant is. A plant that needed water every 5 days in July might only need it every 14 in December.

When you do water, water thoroughly: until water runs out the drainage holes. This rinses out salt buildup and ensures the entire root ball is hydrated, not just the top.

Empty the saucer 15-30 minutes after watering. Roots sitting in a puddle is the fastest path to rot.

Room-temperature water beats cold. Sudden cold can shock tropical roots. Letting tap water sit overnight also lets chlorine dissipate, which sensitive plants like calatheas appreciate.

For plants prone to leaf rot (African violets, gloxinias) or fungus gnats, bottom water: set the pot in a tray of water for 20-30 minutes, let the soil wick water up through the drainage holes, then drain. The top inch of soil stays dry, which suppresses fungus gnat larvae.

Overwatering and underwatering look similar — both cause drooping and yellow leaves. The tells:

- Overwatered: soil stays wet for days, leaves feel soft and limp, stems may be mushy at the base, lower leaves turn yellow first
- Underwatered: soil is bone-dry and pulling away from the pot edges, leaves feel crispy, the pot is very light

When in doubt, check the soil before assuming.
